channel.title: R1V3R5 on R1V3R5: An imaginary journal about fake rivers
channel.description: Recent content in R1V3R5 on R1V3R5: An imaginary journal about fake rivers

item.title: Hyrulean Hydrology: The riddle of Lake Hylia in 'Zelda: A Link to the Past'
item.description:
Introduction #

In The Legend of Zelda: A Link to the Past (ALttP), Lake Hylia receives water from Zora’s River. However, there exists no obvious outflow![1]Zelda Wiki (2026). Lake Hylia This raises two linked (haha) questions: (1) what keeps water level approximately stable, and (2) what prevents long-run dissolved-load accumulation[2]Brutsaert, Wilfried (2005). Hydrology: An Introduction.

We examine the following candidate explanations:

  • H0 (Closed-lake evaporation): Lake Hylia is effectively closed; evaporation balances storage, and observed freshness is only apparent or short-run.
  • H1 (Map-omitted outlet): A conventional outlet exists in-world but is not shown on the map; both water and dissolved load are exported by ordinary flow.
  • H2 (Subsurface outflow): Unmapped subterranean pathways export water and dissolved load from the basin.
  • H3 (Warp-network export): Whirlpool waterways function as hydraulically meaningful export pathways.
  • H4 (Fairy-mediated purification): Fairy activity maintains water quality directly. 1 In its stronger form, the Pond of Happiness acts as a magical sink — a bottomless or magically voiding basin. It removes both water volume and dissolved load, contributing to H3 and to freshness. In that case, H4 is a primary discharge mechanism, not just a quality filter.

Related work #

The most related work is Any Austin’s video series on virtual hydrology, specifically his investigation of rivers in Tears of the Kingdom[3]Any Austin (2025). Where Do Hyrule's Rivers Go?. Austin establishes a route-tracing methodology and finds that all rivers in Tears of the Kingdom end in two lakes: an unnamed small western lake and Lake Hylia. The current consensus places Tears of the Kingdom after ALttP in the Zelda timeline, which may explain the more complex river system and the absence of the giant whirlpool; how much of Austin’s analysis applies to ALttP is unclear.

Initial draft published on 2026-04-17.

No factual corrections have been recorded yet. Open an issue in the repository for map or terminology corrections.

No factual corrections have been made as of 2026-04-14.

If you spot an issue, please open a ticket in the project repository.

item.title: About
item.description: R1V3R5 is still small by design. The intention is to keep the publishing system legible, let the format evolve in public, and publish pieces that treat imaginary rivers seriously enough to require their own editorial home.
